subtopic_detail: Medical admission threshold for severe anorexia nervosa
stem: You are the inpatient psychiatric registrar reviewing a 16-year-old girl referred from her GP. She has restricting-type anorexia nervosa with 30 percent weight loss over 6 months and now refuses any oral intake. She presents with severe bradycardia, postural hypotension and significant electrolyte derangement. She is fully oriented but expresses distorted body image and intense fear of weight gain. She is not Aboriginal or Torres Strait Islander.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 38 supine, BP 80/50 with 30 mmHg postural drop, T 35.6 °C, SpO2 99% room air, BMI 13.5
data_table.Examination: Marked muscle wasting; lanugo hair; cool peripheries; dry mucous membranes; capillary refill 4 seconds
data_table.Investigations: K 2.8 mmol/L, phosphate 0.6 mmol/L, magnesium 0.6 mmol/L, sodium 138 mmol/L, glucose 3.2 mmol/L; ECG QTc 480 ms

lead_in: What is the most appropriate management plan?

options.text: Discharge home with outpatient family-based therapy and weekly weight monitoring
options.rationale: Family-based therapy is the gold standard outpatient model but cannot manage severe bradycardia, postural hypotension and electrolyte derangement; medical admission is required first. (Source: ANZAED Eating Disorders Guidelines)

options.text: Commence olanzapine 5 mg at night and arrange outpatient psychiatry follow-up next week
options.rationale: Olanzapine has only modest evidence in anorexia and does not address medical instability; medical admission and refeeding take precedence. (Source: ANZAED Eating Disorders Guidelines)

options.text: Admit for cardiac monitoring, electrolyte correction and supervised refeeding under refeeding precautions
options.rationale: Bradycardia under 40, postural hypotension, BMI under 14 and hypokalaemia or hypophosphataemia each independently meet admission criteria; supervised refeeding with phosphate and thiamine cover prevents refeeding syndrome. (Source: ANZAED Eating Disorders Guidelines)

options.text: Refer to a dietitian only for outpatient meal planning and review at the GP in 2 weeks
options.rationale: Dietetic input is essential but inadequate alone when medical instability is established; supervised refeeding requires a hospital environment. (Source: ANZAED Eating Disorders Guidelines)

options.text: Discharge with weekly weight checks and contingency plan if she loses more weight at home
options.rationale: Outpatient management is unsafe when admission criteria are met; medical complications including arrhythmia and death are well described in this group. (Source: ANZAED Eating Disorders Guidelines)

explanation.summary: Australian admission criteria for severe anorexia nervosa include any of HR under 40, BP under 80/50, postural drop above 20 mmHg, K under 3.0, phosphate under 0.5, BMI under 14, rapid weight loss or significant ECG changes. Refeeding syndrome risk peaks in the first week of nutritional restoration as intracellular electrolyte shift drives hypophosphataemia, hypomagnesaemia and hypokalaemia. Thiamine 100 mg daily and gradual calorie escalation reduce this risk. Family-based therapy remains the outpatient gold standard once medically stable.
key_points: HR under 40 or BP under 80/50 meets admission criteria.
K under 3.0 or phosphate under 0.5 mandates admission.
Refeeding syndrome peaks in the first week of restoration.
Family-based therapy follows medical stabilisation.
explanation.pearls: Always check phosphate, magnesium and potassium daily for the first week of refeeding; an asymptomatic phosphate of 0.4 on day 3 is the classic harbinger of cardiac arrest in refeeding syndrome and must trigger immediate replacement and pause in calorie advancement.

stem: You are the emergency department psychiatric registrar reviewing a 28-year-old man with a known schizoaffective disorder on quetiapine. His family brings him for 4 days of progressive muteness, immobility and bizarre posturing. He refuses oral intake. He is afebrile, has no muscle rigidity, no autonomic instability and a witnessed episode of waxy flexibility on examination. His creatine kinase is normal. He is not Aboriginal or Torres Strait Islander.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.9 °C, HR 88, BP 122/78, SpO2 99% room air
data_table.Examination: Mute and immobile; maintained postures imposed by examiner (waxy flexibility); no rigidity, no clonus, no tremor; no fever; clinically euvolaemic
data_table.Investigations: FBC normal, U&E normal, CK 110 U/L (normal), thyroid normal, urine drug screen negative; CT brain unremarkable

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Intravenous lorazepam 2 mg challenge with reassessment of catatonic features within 10 minutes
options.rationale: A lorazepam challenge is both diagnostic and therapeutic for catatonia; 60 to 80 percent of patients with catatonia respond within minutes, supporting the diagnosis and guiding ongoing benzodiazepine therapy. (Source: RANZCP Catatonia Guidance)

options.text: Commence ECT immediately as first-line therapy without any trial of benzodiazepine
options.rationale: ECT is highly effective in catatonia but is reserved for patients who fail or cannot tolerate benzodiazepine; the lorazepam challenge precedes ECT in routine pathways. (Source: RANZCP Catatonia Guidance)

options.text: Increase quetiapine dose substantially to address residual psychotic illness in this patient
options.rationale: High-potency antipsychotics can worsen catatonia and risk neuroleptic malignant syndrome; benzodiazepine therapy is preferred. (Source: RANZCP Catatonia Guidance)

options.text: Administer intravenous dantrolene 1 mg/kg now for suspected neuroleptic malignant syndrome
options.rationale: Neuroleptic malignant syndrome requires fever, muscle rigidity and raised creatine kinase; this clinical picture is catatonia, not neuroleptic malignant syndrome. (Source: RANZCP Catatonia Guidance)

options.text: Give intravenous haloperidol 5 mg now for rapid tranquillisation of acute behavioural disturbance
options.rationale: Haloperidol can precipitate or worsen catatonia and risks neuroleptic malignant syndrome; it is not the right agent here. (Source: RANZCP Catatonia Guidance)

explanation.summary: Catatonia is a syndrome of motor immobility, mutism, posturing, waxy flexibility, negativism and stereotypy occurring in mood, psychotic, neurological and medical disorders. Diagnosis is supported by a positive lorazepam challenge: 1 to 2 mg intravenously with reassessment at 5 to 10 minutes typically produces clear improvement. Benzodiazepine therapy is then continued, often in escalating doses. ECT is highly effective for refractory or malignant catatonia and is the standard escalation step when benzodiazepines fail.
key_points: Lorazepam challenge is diagnostic and therapeutic.
Avoid high-potency antipsychotics during catatonia.
Escalate to ECT if lorazepam fails or malignant features develop.
Address underlying medical and psychiatric causes.
explanation.pearls: Malignant catatonia presents with hyperthermia, autonomic instability and raised CK, and overlaps clinically with neuroleptic malignant syndrome; either way, withhold antipsychotics, support physiology and arrange urgent ECT because mortality without rapid treatment exceeds 50 percent.

subtopic_detail: Delusional disorder community management without involuntary admission
stem: You are the community psychiatric registrar reviewing a 52-year-old woman with an 18-month history of a fixed belief that her next-door neighbour is poisoning the water supply to her house. She has no auditory or visual hallucinations, no thought disorder, no negative symptoms and no mood disorder. Her husband reports she works, drives and socialises normally except for this belief. She uses no substances and her mini-mental status examination is normal. She is not Aboriginal or Torres Strait Islander.

data_table.Examination: Cooperative, well-groomed; no thought disorder; circumstantial speech around the persecutory belief; no perceptual disturbance; insight is poor for the belief but preserved elsewhere
data_table.Investigations: TFTs, B12, syphilis serology, HIV negative; urine drug screen negative; MRI brain unremarkable

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next management step?

options.text: Admit involuntarily under the Mental Health Act despite there being no risk of harm to others
options.rationale: Involuntary admission requires risk to self or others or grave disability; isolated non-bizarre delusion with preserved function does not meet the legal threshold. (Source: RANZCP Psychotic Disorders Guidelines)

options.text: Commence sertraline 50 mg daily for likely psychotic depression with predominant delusions
options.rationale: There is no depressive syndrome here; sertraline alone will not treat persistent delusions and may delay appropriate antipsychotic therapy. (Source: RANZCP Psychotic Disorders Guidelines)

options.text: Refer for cognitive behavioural therapy for psychosis alone without any pharmacotherapy
options.rationale: CBT for psychosis is a useful adjunct but is not first-line monotherapy for delusional disorder; antipsychotic therapy is the recommended core intervention. (Source: RANZCP Psychotic Disorders Guidelines)

options.text: Refer for electroconvulsive therapy given treatment resistance and chronicity of belief
options.rationale: ECT is not indicated as first-line for delusional disorder; antipsychotic trial should be undertaken before any ECT consideration. (Source: RANZCP Psychotic Disorders Guidelines)

options.text: Initiate aripiprazole 10 mg daily and arrange outpatient community psychiatric follow-up
options.rationale: An antipsychotic with strong tolerability is reasonable first-line; aripiprazole produces fewer metabolic and prolactin side effects than older agents and supports engagement in long-term community care. (Source: RANZCP Psychotic Disorders Guidelines)

explanation.summary: Delusional disorder is defined by one or more non-bizarre fixed false beliefs for at least 1 month without prominent hallucinations, thought disorder or negative symptoms, and with otherwise preserved functioning. Treatment combines antipsychotic medication with engagement-focused psychotherapy. Response rates to antipsychotics are lower than in schizophrenia but partial improvement supports ongoing care. Involuntary treatment is reserved for cases that meet the relevant Mental Health Act threshold.
key_points: Non-bizarre delusion for at least 1 month defines the disorder.
Antipsychotic is first-line; aripiprazole is well tolerated.
Response is partial; engagement strategies matter.
Involuntary care requires risk or grave disability.
explanation.pearls: The persecutory subtype most often presents to general practice through a third party such as a partner or police; refusal of treatment does not equate to grave disability, and forced admission of well-functioning patients risks harming the long-term therapeutic relationship.

subtopic_detail: Empirical antibiotic therapy for native-valve right-sided endocarditis
stem: You are the medical registrar admitting a 38-year-old man with 4 weeks of fevers, sweats and a 6 kg weight loss. He has injected heroin daily for 4 years. On examination there is a new pansystolic murmur loudest at the lower left sternal edge. Two sets of peripheral blood cultures taken 12 hours apart have already grown methicillin-sensitive Staphylococcus aureus. Transthoracic echo shows an 8 mm tricuspid vegetation. He is not Aboriginal or Torres Strait Islander.

data_table.Vital signs: T 38.4 °C, HR 110, BP 118/72, RR 22, SpO2 96% room air
data_table.Examination: New pansystolic murmur at LLSE louder on inspiration; multiple injection sites on forearms; no peripheral stigmata; clear chest; soft abdomen
data_table.Investigations: Blood cultures: MSSA in 2 of 2 sets at 12 h apart; TTE: 8 mm tricuspid vegetation, preserved LV function; FBC and U&E unremarkable; HIV non-reactive

lead_in: What is the most appropriate antimicrobial therapy?

options.text: Intravenous vancomycin alone for 6 weeks dosed to target trough 15-20 mg/L
options.rationale: Vancomycin is reserved for MRSA or beta-lactam allergy; flucloxacillin has superior outcomes for MSSA infective endocarditis. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines Antibiotic)

options.text: Intravenous flucloxacillin 2 g every 4 hours for at least 4 weeks with daily review
options.rationale: MSSA infective endocarditis on a native valve is treated with intravenous flucloxacillin 2 g every 4 hours for at least 4 weeks; right-sided uncomplicated disease may be eligible for shorter courses with specialist input. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines Antibiotic)

options.text: Intravenous amoxicillin and gentamicin combination for 6 weeks with daily monitoring
options.rationale: Amoxicillin and gentamicin target enterococcal or streptococcal endocarditis; this regimen under-treats Staphylococcus aureus. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines Antibiotic)

options.text: Intravenous ceftriaxone 2 g once daily as monotherapy for 6 weeks under outpatient OPAT
options.rationale: Ceftriaxone has weaker MSSA activity than flucloxacillin and is not recommended as preferred therapy in this setting. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines Antibiotic)

options.text: Oral cephalexin 1 g four times daily with outpatient cardiology review next week
options.rationale: Oral antibiotics are insufficient for native-valve endocarditis; intravenous therapy is required with inpatient initiation. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines Antibiotic)

explanation.summary: Therapeutic Guidelines recommend intravenous flucloxacillin 2 g every 4 hours for native-valve MSSA infective endocarditis for at least 4 weeks, with longer courses for prosthetic valve or complicated disease. Empirical therapy in suspected IE before cultures should cover Staphylococcus aureus (flucloxacillin) and, in prosthetic valve disease or MRSA-prevalent settings, MRSA (vancomycin). All patients need transoesophageal echocardiography for embolic and abscess assessment, daily clinical review, and surgical evaluation for complications.
key_points: Flucloxacillin 2 g 4-hourly is preferred for native-valve MSSA.
Vancomycin is reserved for MRSA or beta-lactam allergy.
Add gentamicin only as advised for synergy in selected scenarios.
TOE is required to assess complications.
explanation.pearls: Right-sided endocarditis in injecting drug users with uncomplicated MSSA disease, no metastatic abscess, normal renal function and no embolic complications can sometimes be treated with shortened intravenous courses or step-down oral therapy under infectious diseases supervision; never make this decision without specialist input.

subtopic_detail: Vasopressor initiation in fluid-refractory septic shock
stem: You are the medical registrar reviewing a 72-year-old man transferred from the ward 48 hours after admission for community-acquired pneumonia. He has now had 2 litres of intravenous crystalloid in the last hour with no improvement. Blood cultures were drawn and intravenous piperacillin-tazobactam was administered 30 minutes ago. He is confused and looks unwell. He is not Aboriginal or Torres Strait Islander.

data_table.Vital signs: T 38.9 °C, HR 124, BP 78/40, RR 28, SpO2 92% on 4 L oxygen, GCS 13
data_table.Examination: Cool peripheries with capillary refill 5 seconds; coarse crackles right base; warm core; oliguria for 2 hours
data_table.Investigations: Lactate 4.2 mmol/L, WCC 21 x10^9/L, CRP 280 mg/L, creatinine 162 micromol/L (baseline 90), procalcitonin elevated

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate next intervention?

options.text: Continue with a further 2 L crystalloid bolus before considering vasopressor support
options.rationale: Persistent hypotension and lactate above 4 after 30 mL/kg of crystalloid (achieved at this weight) means further large boluses risk pulmonary oedema; vasopressors should be started now. (Source: Surviving Sepsis Campaign)

options.text: Commence dopamine infusion as a first-line vasopressor through a peripheral line first
options.rationale: Dopamine is associated with more arrhythmia and worse outcomes than noradrenaline and is no longer recommended as a first-line vasopressor in septic shock. (Source: Surviving Sepsis Campaign)

options.text: Give intravenous hydrocortisone 200 mg before starting any vasopressor infusion now
options.rationale: Hydrocortisone is reserved for vasopressor-refractory septic shock; it does not substitute for vasopressor initiation when hypotension persists after fluid resuscitation. (Source: Surviving Sepsis Campaign)

options.text: Commence noradrenaline infusion through central or large peripheral access targeting MAP ≥65
options.rationale: Fluid-refractory hypotension with lactate above 4 meets septic shock criteria; noradrenaline is the first-line vasopressor in Australian and international guidelines, targeting MAP at least 65. (Source: Surviving Sepsis Campaign)

options.text: Defer further antibiotic doses until repeat blood cultures and sensitivities are available
options.rationale: Antibiotic therapy must continue without interruption once initiated; deferring doses increases mortality in septic shock. (Source: Surviving Sepsis Campaign)

explanation.summary: Surviving Sepsis Campaign hour-1 elements include measuring lactate, drawing blood cultures, administering broad-spectrum antibiotics, giving 30 mL/kg crystalloid for hypotension or lactate above 4, and starting vasopressors if hypotension persists. Noradrenaline is the first-line vasopressor with a MAP target of at least 65 mmHg. Vasopressin and hydrocortisone are added in vasopressor-refractory disease. Source control and re-evaluation drive the next phase of care.
key_points: Lactate above 4 and persistent hypotension after 30 mL/kg meet shock criteria.
Noradrenaline is the first-line vasopressor.
MAP target is at least 65 mmHg.
Add vasopressin and hydrocortisone in refractory shock.
explanation.pearls: In Australian practice, peripheral noradrenaline through a well-placed proximal large bore cannula is safe for at least the first few hours while a central line is being arranged; do not delay vasopressor initiation waiting for a central access in deteriorating septic shock.
